title: Version Control & Git - 50 Words
definition: 50 essential terms for using Git and version control systems to manage source code.

words.meaning: A saved snapshot of changes in a version control system.
words.sentence: I made a commit with the message 'fix login bug'.

words.meaning: Creating a local copy of a remote repository.
words.sentence: I cloned the repository to start working on it locally.

words.meaning: Uploading local commits to a remote repository.
words.sentence: Don't forget to push your changes before ending the day.

words.meaning: Downloading and merging changes from a remote repository.
words.sentence: Pull the latest changes before starting new work.

words.meaning: Downloading updates from a remote repository without merging them.
words.sentence: I fetched the latest changes to see what's new before merging.

words.meaning: A personal copy of someone else's repository.
words.sentence: I forked the open-source project to contribute a fix.

words.meaning: A space where changes are prepared before being committed.
words.sentence: Add these files to the staging area before committing.

words.meaning: The chronological record of all commits made to a repository.
words.sentence: I checked the commit history to see who made that change.

words.meaning: A reference pointing to the current commit in a Git repository.
words.sentence: HEAD is currently pointing to the latest commit on main.

words.meaning: Switching to a different branch or commit in a repository.
words.sentence: I checked out the feature branch to review the changes.

words.meaning: Reapplying commits on top of another base branch.
words.sentence: We rebased our branch onto main to include the latest changes.

words.meaning: Applying a specific commit from one branch onto another.
words.sentence: I cherry-picked the bug fix into the release branch.

words.meaning: A marker used to label a specific point in commit history, often a release.
words.sentence: We tagged this commit as version 2.0.

words.meaning: A version of a repository hosted elsewhere, like on GitHub.
words.sentence: We pushed the changes to the remote repository.

words.meaning: The default name for the remote repository a project was cloned from.
words.sentence: Push your changes to origin main.

words.meaning: A comparison showing the differences between two versions of a file.
words.sentence: Check the diff to see exactly what changed in this commit.

words.meaning: Temporarily saving uncommitted changes to work on something else.
words.sentence: I stashed my changes to quickly fix an urgent bug.

words.meaning: A situation where Git can't automatically combine changes from two branches.
words.sentence: I had to manually resolve a merge conflict in this file.

words.meaning: A command showing who last modified each line of a file.
words.sentence: I used git blame to find out who introduced this bug.

words.meaning: A command displaying the commit history of a repository.
words.sentence: Run git log to see recent commits and their messages.

words.meaning: A merge where the target branch simply moves forward, with no new commit.
words.sentence: Since there were no conflicting changes, Git performed a fast-forward merge.

words.meaning: Combining multiple commits into a single commit.
words.sentence: We squashed our commits into one before merging to main.

words.meaning: A state where HEAD points directly to a commit instead of a branch.
words.sentence: I accidentally entered a detached HEAD state while checking out an old commit.

words.meaning: The original repository a fork was created from.
words.sentence: I synced my fork with the upstream repository.

words.meaning: A file specifying which files Git should not track.
words.sentence: We added the node_modules folder to .gitignore.

words.meaning: A script that runs automatically at specific points in the Git workflow.
words.sentence: A pre-commit git hook runs the linter before allowing a commit.

words.meaning: A repository embedded inside another repository.
words.sentence: This shared library is included as a Git submodule.

words.meaning: A record of updates to branch tips, useful for recovering lost commits.
words.sentence: I used reflog to recover a commit after an accidental reset.

words.meaning: Overwriting remote history with local changes, potentially losing commits.
words.sentence: Be careful with a force push, it can overwrite others' work.

words.meaning: A binary search technique to find the commit that introduced a bug.
words.sentence: We used git bisect to find exactly which commit broke the build.

words.meaning: The local folder containing a project's files with any uncommitted changes.
words.sentence: You have uncommitted changes in your working directory.

words.meaning: The process of examining proposed changes before merging them.
words.sentence: The pull request review flagged a missing test case.

words.meaning: A workflow where developers merge small changes frequently into a shared main branch.
words.sentence: Trunk-based development keeps our main branch always deployable.

words.meaning: A branch created specifically to develop a single feature.
words.sentence: I created a feature branch to build the new search functionality.

words.meaning: An agreed-upon pattern for using branches and merges in a team.
words.sentence: Our Git workflow requires a review before merging to main.

words.meaning: Modifying the most recent commit instead of creating a new one.
words.sentence: I amended the commit to fix a typo in the message.

words.meaning: A terminal application providing a Unix-like command line for Git on Windows.
words.sentence: I run my Git commands through Git Bash on my Windows machine.

words.meaning: Special symbols Git inserts to show conflicting changes needing resolution.
words.sentence: Remove the conflict markers after resolving the merge conflict.

words.meaning: A clone that only includes recent history, not the full repository history.
words.sentence: We used a shallow clone to speed up the CI pipeline.

words.meaning: Extra metadata attached to a Git tag, like a message or author.
words.sentence: We added a Git tag annotation describing the release notes.

words.meaning: Rules preventing direct changes to important branches without review.
words.sentence: Branch protection requires at least one approval before merging to main.

words.meaning: A single repository containing code for multiple projects.
words.sentence: We moved all our services into a single monorepo.

words.meaning: An approach using separate repositories for each project.
words.sentence: We use a polyrepo setup with one repository per microservice.

words.meaning: Software used to interact with Git repositories, command-line or graphical.
words.sentence: I prefer a graphical Git client over the command line for visualizing branches.

words.meaning: A short description explaining what changes a commit contains.
words.sentence: Write a clear commit message so others understand your change.

words.meaning: A conflict that occurs while reapplying commits during a rebase.
words.sentence: I had to resolve a rebase conflict before continuing.

words.meaning: A rule in .gitignore specifying which files or folders to exclude.
words.sentence: We added a git ignore pattern to exclude all .log files.

words.meaning: Ongoing use of version control throughout a project's lifecycle.
words.sentence: Continuous version control ensures we never lose track of changes.

words.meaning: The web address used to clone a specific repository.
words.sentence: Copy the repository clone URL from GitHub to get started.

words.meaning: A command that moves the current branch pointer, optionally undoing commits.
words.sentence: I used git reset to undo the last commit before pushing.

quiz.question: What does 'push' do in Git?
options: Uploads local commits to a remote repository
quiz.answer: Uploads local commits to a remote repository
quiz.explanation: Push sends your local commits to the remote repository.

quiz.question: What is a 'merge conflict'?
options: When Git can't automatically combine changes from two branches
quiz.answer: When Git can't automatically combine changes from two branches
quiz.explanation: Merge conflicts occur when overlapping changes can't be automatically resolved.

quiz.question: What does 'git blame' show?
options: Who last modified each line of a file
quiz.answer: Who last modified each line of a file
quiz.explanation: Git blame attributes each line to the commit and author who last changed it.

quiz.question: A ___ is a personal copy of someone else's repository. (Git term)
quiz.explanation: Forking creates your own copy of another repository to work on independently.

quiz.question: What does 'squash' do in Git?
quiz.explanation: Squashing merges several commits into a single, cleaner commit.
